speaker: interviewer
text: April 9, 1995 with Jack Pomerantz. You were about to tell us about one other close call that you had in the trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: The close call in the truck also was when I was driving with a truck of ammunition. And I had two officers in the truck with me. And when they started to bomb a bridge there, so I couldn't get over the bridge, but I stopped. And the officers went out of the truck and they went-- ran in the woods to save their lives. I couldn't do it because there was the law. We cannot leave the truck with ammunitions just staying by themselves. So when they got out, they both were killed from an explosion. And I was lucky that nothing happened to me. I had to after, turn back with the truck because it took them maybe a day to fix the bridge after it was bombed that we can get over. So the next day, we went over that bridge. And I delivered the ammunition to the line where the military needed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, eventually, you, with the army, ended up back in Lublin, which was near your hometown of Radzyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to come back to that area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's- that's- that's also very important. I'll start out from that point where-- before Lublin. We-- as we were chasing the German army, we're not-- I was away from Ulica, maybe 10, 15 kilometers. And I told my officer. And my officer-- and I told him the story where my family was left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me a jeep, and I went in the jeep. And he told me where they're going to be, you know, approximate. And I went to this Polish woman who was very friendly with my sisters, very close friends. And I asked her what happened to Srulke, Itzel, Genya, and Esther, and also Itzel's children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, all-- and she took me. She showed me a place where they were killed and were buried. And she points out the grave where Gittel-- Genya. [SIGNS] You stay there half an hour. You cry. You pick yourself up. And you're military. You've got to go back. And you went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you have any idea that they had been killed until that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only what she told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she tell you that they died?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me so. They told me that there was a ghetto in Ulica, a little ghetto. My brother Srulke ran away from the ghetto, she said. And they killed him. But looks like he maybe wasn't even killed. He ran away somewheres. But that's what she said. She also said Itzel and his wife, Bella, and, and Gittel and Esther are buried, and she showed me the- the graves. There was no tombstones or anything, no stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she say they--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a little-- that-- oh, the Germans killed them, that the Nazis killed them, and they buried them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in what circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she--

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even think that she knew. She just-- she took me about two miles, and she show it to me. And the fact is, I don't think that any one of them was there. Because if you hear the story further, it seems that she doesn't even know it what happened. Because Genya, after was in-in Lvov, where the sewers of Lvov-- the book. And, and Genya, whilst I met in Lodz after, which we're getting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- her information was incorrect--

speaker: interviewee
text: Incorrect.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at least about Genya.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, I didn't know it. I thought exactly what she told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were looking for your parents also at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That came after. My parents were left in Radzyn. So they were not there at all. So what happened-- and we kept going. [SIGHS] Finally, we lined up not from Radzyn. We didn't go to Lublin yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radzyn. In Radzyn, about 20 kilometers, 30 kilometers from Radzyn, the military told me I got to go to Bialka, which I know the, the place, to pick up potatoes. OK. But I couldn't tell-- we passing by my town, almost the street-- the center street of the town, we're passing to go to Bialka.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. And I couldn't tell my friends in the army not to let me off in the town, but outskirts. Why? Because they were calling me Schweitzer. And the people of the town knew me as Pomerantz. So you were still--

speaker: interviewee
text: It would give me a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still using the identification papers--

speaker: interviewee
text: As Kalman Schweitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from two years ago--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you had gotten as Schweitzer.

speaker: interviewee
text: As Kalman Schweitzer. So I told them to pick me up in the same plays where they left me off. There's no Polish people there. So they left. And I went to my friend Romek Abramski and Vladik Abramski. You're going to see one in the pictures. And he told me everything that happened, that the Germans killed all the Jews, and that the Germans took all the Jews to another town and put them in a ghetto. But my father and mother-- he said he himself was told by the Nazis, by the Germans, to take the father and the mother out on the field, in a place. He took them there. And the-- and that the German told my father to dig a grave for my mother-- dig a hole, a ditch. He wouldn't dig a ditch. So they went, they shot him in the mouth. And they shot my mother there right there. And the Pole had to make the hole to dig. I asked him where it is. He couldn't tell me. He doesn't know. He doesn't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was a witness to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he did it. He digged the hole for them. That's what he told me. And he-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction when he told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My eyes were definitely swollen. I, I almost stopped breathing. I couldn't- It was undescribable, when you hear what happened to your mother and father. And you don't want to express yourself to him too much because I look at him, and I see an [INAUDIBLE] myself. So he gets me there to sleepover.

speaker: interviewer
text: So listen to this. So I say, did anybody survive? He said, six Jews survived. I said, can you take me to them? He says, no, it's late. It's dark. I'll take you tomorrow. And he took me and he gave me a bed to sleep. But I didn't sleep. I-- the way I'm sitting now, I had a machine gun, and I had a pistol. And I was sleeping-- hard to explain you. Every second, I was up. I was sleeping, but I was up. I was personally afraid if I fall asleep, I'd be killed there. And that was in my mind. So I hardly fell asleep. And I got up in the morning. And I asked him, can you take me to these six people who survived? He said, they all were killed by the Polish underground. There was a Polish fascist group who, whatever Hitler didn't do it, they did it. They finished off. What should I tell you? And it had happened just that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: That night the six were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what happened is, when I left him-- I didn't want to talk to him any more and everything. When I left him, I kept walking around. And I kept asking other people, did any Jews survive? Zydzi. Zydzi means Jew. And they all told me there were six of them, but they were killed. Where are they? They wouldn't tell me. Where are the bodies? They wouldn't tell me nothing. OK--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back then with--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back in the army. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --the army? Yeah. And what happened when you got to Lublin? What did you see there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see in Lublin when you got there with the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, now when we're going in-- so the military-- the military-- everything kept moving. Everything was fast. We went into Lublin. I went in in the concentration camp. What I saw is a mountain of little shoes, a mountain of old, older shoes-- bigger people's shoes. I saw-- what you see-- you've been in, in, in Auschwitz? Not in Auschwitz. In Washington, The Holocaust Museum? You could see the same thing what you saw there, exactly. Glasses, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think then? Until then, you knew about people being killed individually.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's a funny thing with me. I'm going-- I'm a different, maybe, type of person. I'm going to explain you why. I was driving a truck, and I had a lot of the stuff all the time in my truck because we came to places where I had cans of food, a lot of stuff. We'd been in the army. Many times at night I was sitting put with a gun to watch over an ammunition-- a big place. They say and they told us, in one day, you said three times, stop, and nobody stops, you shoot. I followed the orders. I had a friend of mine who didn't ask stop. He shoot right away. He would go out on the road and see Germans running away with little wagons. With that, he would machine gun them. I couldn't do it. I went into a town, I see people was cryin' for food. I stopped the truck. I gave out food to German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the only reason I told my friend, do I know if this is the one who kept the killing? Maybe this person is honest, and he didn't kill anybody. You know, and that was my attitude. Was it wrong? I couldn't kill. You follow me?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time, I can say, the Germans say, the government told them to do it. And I say, if I had been in the Israeli army and they would tell me to go kill people for no reason-- children and people-- I would- I would take the gun and give it to my olders. I say, you go do it. I wouldn't do it. So when they say they were told to do it--

speaker: interviewer
text: But the first time that you saw this concentration camp in Lublin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I knew before what happened because when we were coming through, we went into a town, and I saw a mountain going up and down, with people, lined up in the ditch. A couple thousand people were killed just before we got there. I mean, Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had been shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shot, all of them, and covered with a bulldozer with the dirt. And some of them were still living maybe, when the dirt was going. I saw a town--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw it moving up and down because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right. I saw a town. When I came in, I see people-- icicles. They were not even Jews. They were Russians, I think. What happened is, when the Germans-- when the Russians killed a German soldier, they would take out the whole village, no matter how many people would be there, yeah? And they would spray the water at sub-zero-- in the winter, there was 10 below zero-- sprayed with water on them. And they were icicles, frozen and then machine gunned. They were killing the whole village, children, children, parents, oldest. Everybody was killed. And these were not Jewish people. They were Russians because they find one Russian-- one German soldier killed in that town. I also-- when I went through Ukraine, that the Ukraine did it. I got a lot to say about Ukrainian people. Any Polish woman who are married to a-- any Ukrainian woman was married to a Polish man, the Ukrainians would nail them in like a cross to the wall. Because you see, Poland occupied Ukraine. And when the-- when the Germans were there, they were taking revenge on the Poles and on the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the Ukrainians enlisted in the German SS like unbelievable, almost the majority people. They don't like to hear today-- you follow me?-- about it. But that's what they did. The Ukrainian people, they were even some of them worse than the Germans because they did the dirty work for the Germans. I'm interested in knowing-- by- by this time, 1944, then 1945, you were still a young man. You were 26-years-old, 27-years-old. You had seen all of these things. By this time, you had heard how your parents had been murdered. You'd been told at least three or four of your brothers and sisters had been killed. How did you keep going on? How did you manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life meant nothing. Actually, it didn't mean nothing any more. First of all, it came to a time, I was sitting down to eat. You know how I was sitting? On another body in the field when it was a battle. And they were slaying peoples, like flies, on the field. But it was time to eat. So you didn't have where to sit. A lot of the soldiers were sitting on dead bodies. You became like a rock. And you became like-- I don't know how to explain you. The beginning, I was shaking. But as time was going on, you became so immuned-- I don't know if I expressed myself right-- that, you know, if you get a bullet, you expect it any second. You'll be gone. It was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So like, nothing mattered at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it get to a point it didn't. It-- you don't want to die, but you-- I don't know. You learn to accept it. It's already-- because it happened so many years, you know, so many deaths, your friends falling like flies, you know? How many times I went in because they said to go in the pocket if you have a document, to give it to the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you go into the pocket, you find a bunch of diamonds. Would you care for them? I didn't care for them. It didn't mean nothing to me. In a handkerchief, I saw a bunch of diamonds. The guy must've been somewhere in a place where in one of the battles and he find it, whatever. It didn't mean nothing. That didn't mean absolutely nothing. But the, the story where it comes now-- it's the funny story. When the war was over, and I went in what do you say, [POLISH]? When you finish and they give-- in the military when need to rest, what do you call that? After the battle? In English, what would you say? We'll find the word.

speaker: interviewee
text: We call it [POLISH]. It's like, the war is over. And we settled in a place. And everybody asked, do you want to go furlough-- you know, home. I didn't want to go home because everybody was killed. What? Am I going to go to be killed there? So I went to Lublin, me and another friend. The other friend was a one-star general. And as we were in Lublin, there was a brick building. And in the brick building, were sheets of paper with ABC, the alphabet. And if any person-- it was strictly, I think, Jewish. So every Jew would come in there. If his name is Pomerantz, he would look under P.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was looking for survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: For survivors. To see if anybody survived-- would put his name down. My friend tells me, why don't you look? And I said, what am I going to look? I saw in Ulica all these people who were killed. All my family was killed. And I know some of them was still in Russia. So I said there's nothing to look.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as we're walking out. We both agreed, we're going to get a bottle of vodka. And we're maybe going to play roulette. We, we're going to-- we were in a time that time I could have take a machine gun and do anything. Actually, your mind didn't work anymore. It was-- when you come there and you say-- you know that song, [HEBREW]? 'Where should I go?' Yeah, [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- that time, it was like tears and eyes, where do you go? Where do you go? It-it's It cannot explain the feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were ready to play Russian roulette because--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going to take drink, and we were going to do-- and we were-- we were so disgusted. I cannot even bring it out in words, the way we felt. So if you ask me, were you ready to die? It didn't mean nothing. It could have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would have shot yourself in the head at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have been-- we were so bad. Because when we walked out, we don't know where we going. We have 10 days furlough. Where are we going? And as I'm walking, I said to my friend, somebody's calling Yankel. And he says to me, you're really off your rocks. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. I say, no. I hear that's Yankel. But it's so far away, like, like a signal, like an angel would bring you that word, Yankel. You don't know it. And as time goes on, I hear it more and more and louder and louder and louder. And it's-- before I know it's-- I'm sorry-- I see a woman running. And as she comes closer to me, I said, this is the woman, my sister, who was supposed to be dead, who I looked at the grave. While I'm thinking there, I'm laying on the ground. She is on the ground, which I didn't know. The only thing I remember, we woke up in a hospital. We both passed out. She was told-- she told me after, that I was killed by Stalingrad. I was told she is dead. And suddenly, there she is. So she was there with her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Genya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Genya. And we went home. And from the hospital, we made a Shabbos with challah, oh, and fish! Everything was like, like way before the war. Before that happened, this guy-- her husband-- said to me, you're not going back. So what is going to happen? He didn't say. I'm not going to tell you nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before I know, a day or two before, I was told we're going in the woods. And there was a-- so me and my sister and him and that guy-- and that friend of my, who is the one-star general, we're walking in the woods. And there's in one place, you go in, you change the uniform to civilian. And before you know, I'm getting heart beats. I get very hard beats, because I see a big-- a big-- not, not Polish-- a big Russian officer. And he looks at me. And he sees that I'm turning white. And he walks over to me. And he say, don't be scared. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. You understood what he said? With the ghost.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are going where I'm going. Or I'm going where you're going. And we went over there. There was two trucks. And there was a lot of people, quite a few people. And there was crates, big crates, wooden crates. And we went in the trucks. We sat down like that. And the crates went on us. And on the top of us, on the crates, were potatoes. They were keep unloading bags-- material bags of potatoes. And they took us through the border to Czechoslovakia from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is with you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time was my, my friend, my sister, my brother-in-law, and was other people that I don't know who they are. But they all went the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I just want to make clear that even though the person who gave you the information was mistaken about Genya, she was correct that Esther and Itzel and Srulke, they had all been killed? Esther and Itzel and Srulke had all died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and Itzel and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were killed. But that's why it was that shock in- in in Lodz, when here I know she's dead. And here she is. And I-- anyways, from Czechoslovakia, they give us brooms. Like, I think the borders were, were paid off, to my knowledge because, because then, we took a train. It was driving in Czechoslovakia and to the Austrian border. In the Austrian border, there's a bridge. And there's staying a soldier with his chin on his gun and snoring, sleeping. And we were told to take off the shoes and walk through. And we walked through the bridge. And he didn't wake up. I think it was paid off. I don't know. But we went through. We went through, and we got on a train. And we are in Austria. In Austria, in Salzburg-- we were going to go to Germany. In Austria, my sister gets very sick. And if she got so sick that they had to take her off the train, so we went all off the train-- me, my friend, my sister, and my brother-in-law. And they took us to a camp called Reidenberg. And-- No, I'm sorry. I'm sorry. Parsch was the camp, Parsch. And there was barracks, brick barracks with apartments, like condominiums. And there already must've been there about 500 families living in the condominiums, all refugees. And Salzburg at that time was actually a transfer area from people went different places. People went to Israel. People went-- At that time, Jews couldn't go to Israel because England would let them in. So my sister went in in the hospital, and she got very sick. She got sick that that doctor told me he didn't even know if she's going to survive. And he mentioned to me sicknesses that's not even the bug that she has. I find out later it's because she was in the sewer. That's why the bug from the sewer. When they confiscated the ghetto in Lvov, a group of people went in to live in the sewer for 18 months. And many times, they were in the water up to their neck, as a big storm came. So she was there. And the- and the doctor told me that she-- whatever she eats, she throws up. I should try to get her chicken soup. I don't know how he knew I'm Jewish because when he asked me for chicken soup, I assumed things only Jewish-- I assumed only Jews had chicken soup. I got up in the... in the morning, and I went to a farmer, and I wanted to buy a chicken. And he asked me, if you want to buy a chicken, you should come in the evening because then, I can stop the chickens goin' out in the morning. I can get it. But in the morning, they're out already in the field. I cannot catch it. So I got back in the morning, and I got a kitchen-- the chicken. And I took the chicken. And I made it, and I brought her the food. And the girl that you see in the picture, the one I married, that was the farmer's daughter. And that time, they invited me for Christmas. And I told them, I'm going to come home for Christmas only if I can get some liquor to bring with me. They misunderstood me. And they thought I meant, if I don't have the liquor, I-I don't have to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're young. Again, I'm going to repeat. When you're young, you're a little stupid. [LAUGHS] So I had the liquor. But it bothered me, why they didn't tell me, if you don't have the liquor, you can come too. So 10 times I kept going there and went back, went there and went back because I was sensitive towards the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, I opened the bottle of liquor. I got drunk. And I fell asleep in my clothes. And I never went there. I went there in the morning. They were ready to kill me because they were so upset. They were waiting hours for me to come, and I never got there. And I told them after why. And they said, I'm silly. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once again, another close call. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Another--

speaker: interviewer
text: --not as serious.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kept going there. And that's it. And I moved in. And I lived there in the.. with the farmer, with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's continue in one minute.
